event: Christ Appears to John and Commands Him to Send the Book to the Seven Churches of Asia
symbols: Christ standing with staff and book — the risen Lord who holds the authority of heaven and commissions the prophetic word
seven stars surrounding Christ — the angels of the seven churches (Revelation 1:20), held in Christ's sovereign care
John prostrate at Christ's feet — the seer overwhelmed by the vision of glory, falling as though dead (Revelation 1:17)
the angel in green and gold — the heavenly mediator conveying the revelation
the seven horseshoe arches in the lower register — the seven churches of Asia to which the book is sent (Revelation 1:11)
John writing the book in the lower register — the apostle fulfilling Christ's command to 'write what you see' (Revelation 1:19)
the Mozarabic horseshoe arches — the distinctive visual language of the Beatus manuscript tradition from medieval Iberia
description: A Mozarabic illuminated manuscript page divided into two registers. In the upper register, a haloed Christ stands at center in dark robes holding a tall staff and a book, surrounded by seven eight-pointed stars against a dark blue background. To the left, the apostle John lies prostrate at Christ's feet. To the right, a winged angel in green and gold gestures toward Christ. Above them, a row of decorative horseshoe arches in red and gold frames the top of the page.
theological_description: This folio from a Beatus Apocalypse manuscript depicts the opening vision of the Book of Revelation (Revelation 1:9-20), when the exiled John on Patmos hears a voice 'like a trumpet' and turns to see the risen Christ standing among seven golden lampstands. The Mozarabic artistic tradition, flourishing in Christian communities under Islamic rule in medieval Iberia, developed a uniquely vivid visual theology of the Apocalypse through the commentary of Beatus of Liebana (c. 776). Christ holding the book and staff embodies His dual authority as revealer and ruler -- He alone is worthy to open the sealed scroll (Revelation 5:9). The seven stars are identified by Christ Himself as 'the angels of the seven churches' (Revelation 1:20), signifying His sovereign oversight of the universal Church. John's prostration expresses the proper human response to theophany, echoing Daniel falling on his face before the heavenly vision (Daniel 10:9) and Ezekiel overwhelmed by the glory of the Lord (Ezekiel 1:28). Christ's command -- 'Write what you see in a book and send it to the seven churches' (Revelation 1:11) -- is shown fulfilled in the lower register where John writes. The seven arches representing the seven churches (Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea) visualize the particular-yet-universal scope of the prophetic message: addressed to specific congregations, it speaks to the whole Church across all ages.

scripture_refs.reason: John's inaugural vision of the risen Christ on Patmos, the direct narrative source for the upper register: Christ among the stars, John prostrate.
Christ's command to write what you see and send it to the seven churches -- depicted in the lower register with John writing and the seven arches.
Christ's explanation that the seven stars are the angels of the seven churches and the seven lampstands are the churches themselves.
The seven letters to the seven churches of Asia, represented by the seven labeled arches in the lower register.
Daniel fell into a deep sleep on his face at the heavenly vision, prefiguring John's prostration before the glorified Christ.
Ezekiel fell on his face before the glory of the Lord, the prophetic precedent for John's overwhelmed response.
Christ alone is worthy to open the scroll -- His authority over the book connects to the staff and book He holds in this image.

_meta.title: Le Christ se penche vers Jean. Jean envoie le Livre aux Sept Eglises d'Asie
